text: The robotic arm pushes the cecum cranially while detaching the mesenteric root using a bottom-to-up approach along the Gerota's fascia. The surgeon uses bipolar forceps and a monopolar hook for dissection, continuing cranially until the third portion of the duodenum is visible. The last ileal loop is displaced caudally, and the transverse mesocolon is pushed cranially to expose the superior mesenteric axis. The visceral peritoneum is opened, revealing the previously developed plane, with a clear frontal view of the superior mesenteric vessels.

The surgeon dissects and sections the ileocecal vein between clips, followed by division of the ileocecal artery located posteriorly to the superior mesenteric vein. The dissection progresses upwards, dividing the middle colic vein and the right branch of the middle colic artery at their roots. The complete mesocolic excision exposes the anterior aspect of the superior mesenteric vein, revealing the final view of the vascular anatomy.

A sponge is placed beneath the transverse mesocolon to delineate the surgical plane and protect the duodenum. The surgeon mobilizes the colon and transects the greater omentum. After assessing bowel perfusion with ICG-enhanced fluorescence, the transverse colon is divided using a 45mm blue cartridge robotic stapler. The terminal ileum is also divided. Small enterotomies are created with a monopolar hook, followed by a stapled side-to-side ileocolic anastomosis. The enterotomies are closed with a double-layer barbed Romney suture.
The specimen is extracted through a small incision created by connecting the two paramedian suprapubic trocars. The final view shows the specimen with the area of mesocolic excision clearly visible.
